summary.intent: Migrate a React Native/Expo app to Electron with file-based routing, add a CheckpointTimeline screen, and fix live-update issues in the checkpoints UI by wiring up the server's WebSocket broadcast to V2 checkpoint tables.
summary.outcome: Successfully migrated the app from Expo to Electron+Vite+React Router, added expo-router then replaced it, created the CheckpointTimeline screen, fixed the WebSocket broadcast to watch CheckpointMetadata, and refactored the sessions view to use CheckpointSessionMetadata with branch and intent fields from V2 tables.

repo: The project is at /Users/stephanostsoucas/gossamer with an `app/` subdirectory for the frontend and `src/server.ts` for the API server.
The server uses Prisma with both legacy tables (Session, Event, CheckpointSession) and V2 tables (CheckpointMetadata, CheckpointSessionMetadata, CheckpointSessionSummary).
The WebSocket broadcast poller only watched the `Event` table — CheckpointMetadata inserts were silent, causing the UI not to update.
CheckpointSessionMetadata has branch indexed by sessionId; CheckpointSessionSummary has intent (accessed via summary.intent).
The git worktree at /tmp/gossamer-checkpoints gets cleared by macOS between restarts, causing a non-fatal startup warning.
electron-vite outputs to `out/` by default, not `dist-electron/` — the main field in package.json must be `out/main/index.js`.
expo-router uses `app/` as default routes directory, which conflicts with the project already being in an `app/` folder; fixed via `["expo-router", { "root": "routes" }]` in app.json.

code.finding: WebSocket broadcast poller only watched Event table max id; needed to also track CheckpointMetadata max id to trigger live updates when checkpoints are indexed.
runIndex() was only called after a 30s delay on startup; changed to call immediately so checkpoints appear right away.
Sessions list endpoint needed to join CheckpointSessionMetadata (for branch) and CheckpointSessionSummary (for intent) to serve V2 data instead of legacy session fields.

workflow: Background tasks with exit code 144 are expected kills from pkill during server restarts — not real failures.
electron-vite build is fast and catches TypeScript errors cleanly; use `electron-vite build` before restarting the server.
A primitives.tsx shim (View→div, Text→div, TouchableOpacity→div[role=button]) makes React Native → web migration mechanical with minimal screen-level changes.
MemoryRouter is appropriate for Electron desktop apps (no URL bar); title management via useLocation().state passed through navigate() replaces Stack.Screen options.
Stale background task failure notifications pile up when servers are killed by pkill — safe to ignore exit code 144.

friction: expo-router naming collision with the existing app/ project directory required discovering and using the undocumented `root` plugin option.
electron-vite default output path (`out/`) didn't match the assumed path (`dist-electron/`), requiring a fix to package.json main field before Electron would launch.
macOS clears /tmp between reboots, so the git worktree registered at /tmp/gossamer-checkpoints causes a non-fatal warning on every server restart.
Background task output files in /tmp get cleared by macOS, making it impossible to read output from older completed tasks.
Stale background task failure notifications continued arriving throughout the session, requiring repeated triage to confirm they were harmless kills.
open_items: Branch field is not stored on the Session model — it's derived from CheckpointSessionMetadata; if a session has no checkpoint, branch shows as null.
The worktree prune fix (to suppress the startup warning about missing /tmp/gossamer-checkpoints) was edited into server.ts but the session ended before verifying it works cleanly.
The Electron app's Mantine table for sessions was partially styled but the full Mantine library integration was not confirmed as installed/configured.
WebSocket broadcast now includes CheckpointMetadata changes, but there's no confirmation that the Electron renderer's WebSocket connection is actually receiving and reacting to these broadcasts.
The CheckpointTimeline screen uses a branch-name hash for colors but branch data comes from CheckpointSessionMetadata — no verification that branch is correctly populated in the timeline view.
